translation: Whatever is in the heavens and earth exalts Allah, and He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

This Surah belongs to the Musabi'hat, the collection of surahs that begin with glorifying Allah ﷻ. Understanding this opening requires us to grasp a profound mode of argument that runs through these surahs. Allah ﷻ tells us that all of creation declares His perfection, and then He addresses believers about their shortcomings. This creates a powerful comparison: if the entire universe fulfills its purpose of glorifying Allah ﷻ, what about us?

We need to understand three levels of comparison here. Allah ﷻ already told us in the Makkan Quran that the best of all creation is the human being: *"We have certainly honored the children of Adam."* We enjoy a special status among all creation. Yet when it comes to declaring tasbih, declaring Allah's perfection, Allah ﷻ says that all other creation declares His perfection. If we are the best of all creation, then who should be doing the best tasbih? We should be. Not only is this right not being fulfilled best by human beings in general, but even among humans, the believers carry the heaviest burden. You are a human being, so you already owe more tasbih than all other creation. But on top of that, you are also a believer, so the burden is even heavier on you.

This is how the argument works throughout these surahs: first, Allah ﷻ says the entire universe is doing what it is supposed to do. Then He asks, what happened to you? You are a believer. There is this constant comparison between the universe fulfilling its role with Allah ﷻ, and us as believers and our community fulfilling our role with Him.

Some surahs begin with *sabbaha lillah* (in the past tense, indicating continuous action): everything has been declaring the perfection of Allah ﷻ. Let's explore this concept of tasbih and how it differs between human beings and the rest of creation.

You can tell the talent of a painter by looking at the painting without ever meeting the painter. Similarly, you can observe what Allah ﷻ created—just the sky, the mountains, the earth—and the creation itself becomes a praise of Allah ﷻ. It declares His perfection. Simply observing it makes you think: if this is so beautiful, how incredible must be the One Who made it? This is tasbih by existence, the glorification that happens through the grandeur and diversity of Allah's creation.

On another level, every creation—inanimate objects, animals, insects, cells, atoms, subatomic particles—all have been given a language by which they communicate the perfection of Allah ﷻ. As Allah ﷻ says, *"There is nothing in existence except that it continues to declare the perfection of Allah by doing His hamd. However, you cannot understand their tasbih."* Notice that Allah ﷻ used the word *yusabbihu bihamdihi*—"they glorify Him with His praise"—and then said *"lā tafqahūna tasbīḥahum"* (you do not understand their tasbih). He did not say *lā tafqahūna tasbīḥahā* (using the feminine pronoun for "thing"). He used the masculine plural *hum*, which is used for creatures with intellect. He essentially made them animate, because *hum* is what you use for beings with will. They are doing something that beings with intellect and will do—they are doing His hamd.

This is where the Surah begins: everything has been declaring the perfection of Allah ﷻ in the skies and on the earth. *Wa Huwa al-'Azīzu al-Ḥakīm*—and He is the Ultimate Authority, the All-Wise.

The first six ayahs of this Surah reintroduce Muslims to who God is—pure praise of Allah ﷻ, His grandeur, His greatness. Before we address the subject matter of weak iman, we need to understand why. Perhaps the weakness of our iman is directly related to the fact that we are not appreciating the greatness of Allah ﷻ enough. Before we address that problem, we should go straight to the solution. The first solution offered in this Surah for weakness of iman is declaring Allah's perfection and observing the rest of the universe around us. Going back to the basics.

translation: His is the dominion of the heavens and earth. He gives life and causes death, and He is over all things competent
commentary:
لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

To Him alone belongs the dominion, the kingdom of the skies and the earth. *Yuḥyī wa yumīt*—He gives life and He gives death. *Wa Huwa 'alā kulli shay'in Qadīr*—and He is in complete control over all things.

translation: He is the First and the Last, the Ascendant and the Intimate, and He is, of all things, Knowing
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلْأَوَّلُ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرُ وَٱلظَّـٰهِرُ وَٱلْبَاطِنُ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

This is an incredibly profound ayah that scholars of aqidah and philosophers have grappled with for centuries. I will not delve into those philosophical discussions here, because when you discuss philosophy, you risk sucking the soul out of Islam. Those who are interested and inclined can study those perspectives on your own and reach your conclusions, insha'Allah.

What I will highlight is something unique about how Allah ﷻ mentions His names here. Usually, when Allah ﷻ mentions His names, He does not put a *waw* (and) between them. He does not say "Al-'Azīz *wa* Al-Ḥakīm." But look at what He says here: *Huwa al-Awwal wa al-Ākhir wa adh-Dhāhir wa al-Bāṭin*. This is unique because all of these names are opposites—they are anti-names.

He is the First and the Last. He is before all was, and He will be after all is gone. *Wa adh-Dhāhir*—and He is the Obvious One. There is no reality more obvious than Allah ﷻ Himself. He is the ultimate reality. *Wa al-Bāṭin*—and there is no greater entity hidden than Allah ﷻ Himself. He is the most obvious and the most hidden at the same time.

For the one who observes Allah's creation and reflects with the mind that Allah ﷻ has given them, this will become the most obvious reality that has ever been there for them. Yet at the same time, Allah ﷻ is in the unseen from us. So He is both of those things simultaneously.

*Wa Huwa bikulli shay'in 'Alīm*—and He is knowledgeable over all things. Notice that knowledge will come up repeatedly in what follows.

translation: It is He who created the heavens and earth in six days and then established Himself above the Throne. He knows what penetrates into the earth and what emerges from it and what descends from the heaven and what ascends therein; and He is with you wherever you are. And Allah, of what you do, is Seeing
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا يَلِجُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا يَخْرُجُ مِنْهَا وَمَا يَنزِلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا يَعْرُجُ فِيهَا ۖ وَهُوَ مَعَكُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ

*Huwa alladhī khalaqa as-samāwāti wa al-arḍa fī sittati ayyām*—He is the One Who created the skies and the earth in six days. *Thumma istawā 'alā al-'Arsh*—then He rose upon the Throne, He took His place, as we have discussed before.

*Ya'lamu mā yaliju fī al-arḍ*—He knows what enters into the earth. *Wa mā yakhruju minhā*—and what comes out of it. What enters into the earth? The plant. The corpse, the grave, the coffin. The rain. The seed. And whatever comes out of the earth—on Judgment Day it will be us, but before that it is plants, treasures, resources.

*Wa mā yanzilu mina as-samā'*—and He knows what comes down from the sky. *Wa mā ya'ruju fīhā*—and what rises up into the sky. What rises up into the sky is not just water vapor. It is also the deeds of human beings on a daily basis. It is also our souls.

*Wa Huwa ma'akum ayna mā kuntum*—and He is with you wherever you may be.

This ma'iyyah of Allah ﷻ, this concept of His being with us—when we became influenced by Greek philosophy, we started asking: Is Allah ﷻ physically here with us? How is He here with us? If He says He is with us here, where is He exactly? He says He is on the Throne. He says He is with us. How do we reconcile these things? We are missing the entire point.

The point of Allah ﷻ saying *Wa Huwa ma'akum* is to know that you are never alone. It is to know that Allah's company is with you. It is to know that you and I are constantly in Allah's presence.

First, Allah ﷻ describes that He is this incredible King Who rose on His Throne, Who created everything, and everything else is in submission to Him. Now pause for a moment and consider the psychological impact. You are being told about this important Entity that everybody has respect for, Who has this grand kingdom, this incredibly important Figure. Then suddenly you are told: He is right there next to you.

What happens to you? Are you not shaken? Wait, He is here? He is with me? Does your behavior experience a change if you realize Someone of that value, that importance, that grandeur, that magnitude is there?

And by the way, if an important person at the human level—if that important a person was with you personally, ready to listen to you—would you not make whatever requests you possibly could? Wouldn't you?

Allah ﷻ is the One Who offers His ma'iyyah, His closeness. Where I personally take insight from this in a Surah addressing the weakness of iman: perhaps when the realization that Allah ﷻ is accompanying us in our lives, that Allah's ma'iyyah, His closeness, is with us all the time—when that recognition starts getting diluted, our iman starts getting diluted. Our faith, our conviction, our struggle, the zeal to struggle, starts getting diluted.

This Surah will also provide a comprehensive view of the reality of spirituality—the real essence of spirituality. This Surah, to me at least, addresses that issue in the most comprehensive way. What real spirituality is and what fake spirituality is—it actually addresses both of those in a very powerful way. But now, in the beginning, this iman in Allah ﷻ, which is at the heart of our faith, is being established.

*Wa Allāhu bimā ta'malūna Baṣīr*—and Allah ﷻ, in regards to everything that you are doing, is in full view.

translation: His is the dominion of the heavens and earth. And to Allah are returned [all] matters
commentary:
لَّهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ

*Lahu mulku as-samāwāti wa al-arḍ*—to Him alone belongs the kingdom of the skies and the earth. Has this already been mentioned? Yes, it has. Now it is being mentioned again.

First it was mentioned without mention of you. Then you were mentioned: He is with you, and He is watching everything you are doing. Now let me remind you again: He is the King of the skies and the earth. The King is watching you. He has His attention on you.

*Wa ilā Allāhi turja'u al-umūr*—and to Allah ﷻ all matters, all decisions, are going to be taken back.

This phrase interestingly combines two things: the decision and the action. Somebody decides to do something but does not get to do it. A person decides to go to Hajj, but Allah ﷻ takes their life before they get to go. That decision is taken back to Allah ﷻ. That is an *amr* too—that is also a matter.

*Umūr* is not just actions. That is why the mercy of Allah ﷻ is so vast. It includes not just actions but also intentions. All of it is included. Someone intends to do good—they did good, but their intention was not good. Their intention was not good. There are so many things you can do for Allah ﷻ but for shallow reasons: popularity, appreciation, fame, career advancement. It could look like you are providing a religious service, but you are actually in it for something much more pathetic.

That decision you made internally, that nobody knows about—nobody can question your intentions. Only Allah ﷻ knows your intentions. Nobody can tell what your real motives are, but Allah ﷻ does. And those *umūr* are all taken back to Him.

This is why we will see in the coming ayahs that Allah ﷻ mentions iman and infaq together. Infaq is a voluntary action. Breathing is a *fi'l* (action), but it is involuntary. Walking—one leg, then the other—that is a *fi'l*. You do not have to consciously say, "Time for the left leg." You do not do that. If you did, that would be an *amr*, a conscious command. That would be speech.

For some people who do not think about what they are saying, speaking is a *fi'l*—they just run their mouth. For others, speaking is an *amr*—a conscious action based on intent.

Breathing or opening your eyes and seeing—seeing is not an *amr*, but looking is. Hearing is not an *amr*, but listening is. I cannot turn my ears off if somebody is blasting music or a dog is barking. I cannot choose not to hear. You hear everything. But what you pay attention to and listen to—now that is an *amr*.

So when Allah ﷻ says He is in full view of your *amr* and *'amal*—the intent and the act together—He is watching what you intended and what you did. This is not just about having evil intentions. This is also about having weak intentions.

Weak intentions are an equally big problem for Muslims. "I want to memorize the Quran." Okay, what have you done for it? "I intend to memorize the Quran." Great. What are you going to do about it? "The intention is there at least, so the reward is coming." Yeah, when is that *niyyah* going to translate into some action? Get to work.

One of my favorite places in the Quran when it comes to dua is: *Rabbanā ātinā fī ad-dunyā ḥasanatan wa fī al-ākhirati ḥasanatan*—Our Master, give us goodness in this world and goodness in the Hereafter. The very next ayah says: *Ulā'ika lahum naṣībun mimmā kasabū*—those are the people who are going to get a portion from what they actually earned.

What that means is this: when you make dua to Allah ﷻ—"Allah, give me goodness in this world, goodness in the Hereafter"—Allah ﷻ opens the doors for you to earn that goodness. If you earn it, then you will get it. The dua will not get you there. The dua will open the doors for you. Now you have to act. You have to do what you are supposed to do.

translation: He causes the night to enter the day and causes the day to enter the night, and he is Knowing of that within the breasts
commentary:
يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ

*Yūliju al-layla fī an-nahār wa yūliju an-nahāra fī al-layl*—He inserts the night into the day and the day into the night.

You just realized that all deeds are being taken back to Him. Then He says He is the One making the clock of the earth—the night and day. That is our clock. He is making the hours pass. He is making the days go one after the other.

The point is to make you conscious: Days are going by. Have I changed? What deeds are being reported to Allah ﷻ as the night is going into the day? What deeds are being reported of mine to Allah ﷻ as the day is going into the night? These are not just abstract philosophies. They change your perception of what your relationship and my relationship with Allah ﷻ is supposed to be.

*Wa Huwa 'Alīmun bidhāti aṣ-ṣudūr*—and He is knowledgeable, fully knowledgeable, of the nature of the chests. *Dhāt* means the original state of something, the nature of something, the personality of something. *Ṣudūr* actually means chests, but it refers to sentiments, feelings, conscience.

Allah ﷻ knows me better than I know myself. Allah ﷻ even knows my subconscious. People have behaviors, and they go to a therapist who brings out something from their subconscious they did not even know about themselves. They go through hypnosis and all this other stuff. People have repressed memories. They have nightmares and do not know where they came from. They have dreams they cannot understand.

Allah ﷻ knows what is going on in your conscious mind and your subconscious. He knows you better than you know your own self.

This incredible transition I find fascinating in these ayahs: on the one hand, Allah ﷻ is the King of an endless universe—*as-samāwāti wa al-arḍ*. We appreciate the size of the universe more now than perhaps ever before. He has control over that vast expanse. Then He is talking about these neurons firing in our brain that we are not even aware of, that we are not even conscious of. At that microscopic level, He knows. *Wa Huwa 'Alīmun bidhāti aṣ-ṣudūr*.

translation: Believe in Allah and His Messenger and spend out of that in which He has made you successors. For those who have believed among you and spent, there will be a great reward
commentary:
ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَأَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا جَعَلَكُم مُّسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ

*Āminū billāhi wa Rasūlih*—believe in Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ.

Now we come to the actual address to the believers. Remember, who is the primary audience? Muslims. Look at this command form: *Āminū billāhi wa Rasūlih*—Muslims are being told, "Believe in Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ."

This is a very significant subject in the Quran. There are two levels of iman. This command to believe, given to those who already believe, points to something profound about the nature of faith—it must be renewed, strengthened and deepened continuously.

translation: And why do you not believe in Allah while the Messenger invites you to believe in your Lord and He has taken your covenant, if you should [truly] be believers
commentary:
وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ ۙ وَٱلرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِرَبِّكُمْ وَقَدْ أَخَذَ مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

**What is wrong with you that you do not believe in Allah, while the Messenger invites you to believe in your Lord, and He has already taken your covenant, if you should be believers?**

### The Challenge: What's Wrong With You?

Allah ﷻ confronts us directly: وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ—what is wrong with you that you do not believe in Allah?). This phrase وَمَا لَكُمْ (what's wrong with you?) is a powerful rebuke.

The expression لَا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ has two dimensions in Arabic. It can mean "you don't believe in Allah," but it also carries the meaning "you don't believe Allah." What's the difference?

When you say "I believe in Allah," you affirm His existence, His lordship, His names and attributes, His uniqueness. When you say "I believe Allah," you express complete conviction, confidence and trust in what He tells you. In the previous ayah, Allah ﷻ made a promise: Give Me your youth, your money, your talent, your aspirations, your goals—and I will give you a huge compensation. Now He asks: What's wrong with you? Don't you believe Me? Don't you trust what I'm telling you?

### The Messenger Invites You

وَٱلرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِرَبِّكُمْ (while the Messenger invites you to believe in your Lord). The Messenger ﷺ himself is actively calling you, inviting you to truly believe in your Rabb—your Master, your Sustainer, the One who nurtures and cares for you.

What more motivation could you possibly need? The Prophet ﷺ is personally inviting you to this elevated state of faith.

### Remembering the Original Covenant

وَقَدْ أَخَذَ مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (and He has already taken your covenant, if you should be believers).

How is this something new? The real contract was taken from you long before the Messenger ﷺ even came. When you were created, when all souls were brought forth and Allah ﷻ asked, "Am I not your Lord?" and they responded, "Yes, we testify" (7:172)—that was the original agreement. Believing now, spending now, responding to the call now—this is simply reaffirming and acting upon that primordial covenant.

If you truly consider yourself among the believers, remember: you already agreed to this.

translation: It is He who sends down upon His Servant [Muhammad] verses of clear evidence that He may bring you out from darknesses into the light. And indeed, Allah is to you Kind and Merciful
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُنَزِّلُ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦٓ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ لِّيُخْرِجَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ

**It is He who sends down upon His servant clear verses that He may bring you out from darknesses into the light. And indeed, Allah is to you Kind and Merciful.**

### Continuous Revelation and Support

A natural question arises: If someone does experience that surge of Iman and commits to spending for Allah's sake, how can they maintain it? Can someone who steps into the light be pulled back into darkness? Unfortunately, yes—this happens all the time. We all experience good days and bad days in our faith.

Allah ﷻ provides the answer: هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُنَزِّلُ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦٓ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ (He is the One who sends down upon His slave clear verses). The verb يُنَزِّلُ  is in the present tense, indicating continuous, repeated action. Allah ﷻ doesn't just send revelation once. He continuously sends down, over and over again, miraculous signs and clear revelations to His servant (the Prophet ﷺ and, by extension, to all who receive this guidance).

Why? لِّيُخْرِجَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ (lso He may bring you out from darknesses into the light). Notice that "darknesses" is plural (ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ) while "light" is singular (ٱلنُّورِ). There are countless forms of darkness—ignorance, doubt, sin, confusion, despair—but there is only one true light: the guidance of Allah ﷻ.

The continuous revelation ensures that when you slip back into darkness, the Quran is there to pull you back out. When your faith weakens, the words of Allah ﷻ are there to revive it.

### Allah's Intimate Knowledge of Your Struggle

وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (and indeed, Allah is to you Kind and Merciful).

The word رَءُوف  is particularly meaningful here. While رَحِيم  means merciful, رَءُوف  conveys something deeper—it means to feel and identify with someone's pain. When you try to explain your struggles to someone and they respond with indifference, that's a lack of رأفة . They don't truly understand what you're going through.

Allah ﷻ calls Himself رَءُوف, affirming that He doesn't just show mercy from a distance. He knows exactly what you're experiencing. He identifies completely with your state, your struggles, your challenges in maintaining faith. He understands the battle you face between spiritual aspirations and worldly responsibilities.

Specifically "to you" (بِكُمْ), Allah ﷻ is compassionate. In your particular case, with your unique circumstances, Allah ﷻ is intimately aware and constantly ready to shower His mercy and blessings upon you.

translation: And why do you not spend in the cause of Allah while to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth? Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest [of Makkah] and fought [and those who did so after it]. Those are greater in degree than they who spent afterwards and fought. But to all Allah has promised the best [reward]. And Allah, with what you do, is Acquainted
commentary:
وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تُنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِلَّهِ مِيرَٰثُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ لَا يَسْتَوِى مِنكُم مَّنْ أَنفَقَ مِن قَبْلِ ٱلْفَتْحِ وَقَـٰتَلَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنفَقُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدُ وَقَـٰتَلُوا۟ ۚ وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ

**And why should you not spend in the cause of Allah while to Allah belongs the inheritance of the heavens and the earth? Not equal among you are those who spent and fought before the conquest and those who spent and fought afterwards. The former are greater in degree than the latter. But to all Allah has promised the best reward. And Allah is All-Aware of what you do.**

### Everything Returns to Allah

وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تُنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ (and why should you not spend in the cause of Allah?). Again, the challenging question: What's wrong with you that you wouldn't spend?

وَلِلَّهِ مِيرَٰثُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (while to Allah belongs the inheritance of the heavens and the earth). Have you forgotten that Allah ﷻ alone owns the ultimate inheritance of everything? The word مِيرَٰثُ  means inheritance—what remains after someone passes away.

What are you holding onto? Those few dollars? That little bit of time? That youth? Don't you realize that even you are His property? He owns you. You came from the earth and you will return to it. What will happen to that car you were saving for instead of serving the religion? That house you prioritized over Allah's path? Everything you possess will be left behind, and it all ultimately belongs to Him.

### A Reality Check for Modern Believers

This conversation isn't abstract history about the Companions (RA). This is about you and me, here and now. Many of us have experienced that initial fire when we first truly connected with Islam. I know someone who became Muslim and immediately became extremely active—he just wanted to serve Allah ﷻ however he could. He got into a serious accident once and thought he was going to die. The only thoughts running through his mind were: Have I done enough for the Quran? Have I served enough? He wasn't thinking about his wife or kids in those moments, because a deeper truth had gripped him: Allah ﷻ will take care of them. Provision is not on me; provision is on the Provider. That was his mentality.

But he survived the accident. He graduated, got a job, then another contract, then another. Career, career, career. He told me recently: "For the last eight or nine years, all I've been thinking about is savings, investment, the next move, the promotion, saving for the kids, paying off the house, real estate, retirement accounts. Yes, I pray and attend the mosque, but my day and night hasn't been spent thinking, 'How am I going to get to Jannah?' It hasn't been like that. I remember being that person once. I'm not that person anymore. I don't know what happened. I'm starting to question why I'm on this earth—just to save more money and build a career?"

Taking care of your family and having a good career is not wrong. But when that's what your life amounts to, when that becomes your entire purpose—that's the question each of us must ask ourselves privately. Are we living to work, or are we working to serve Allah ﷻ?

Some people just live to work. If they lose their job, they've lost their dignity, their purpose in life. That job was their entire identity. Are we those people? Are we just cogs in a machine, parts of a factory? Is our sole purpose in this economy to save money, buy a house, pay it off, raise children, send them to college, and then exit this world? Making some prayers along the way if we can manage? Or is there something more?

### Spending Before Victory vs. After

لَا يَسْتَوِى مِنكُم مَّنْ أَنفَقَ مِن قَبْلِ ٱلْفَتْحِ وَقَـٰتَلَ (not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest and fought). Allah ﷻ speaks of victory (الْفَتْحِ) because by the time this surah was revealed, a major victory had already occurred.

Those who spent for Islam's sake when there was no hope in sight, when everything around them gave reason for pessimism, when things were getting worse by the day—those people hold a different rank. أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً (they are greater in degree) than those who spent and fought after victory came, when Islam was established and the challenges had already been met.

This is our golden opportunity. When we look at the state of our Ummah today, both here and around the world, we must acknowledge that we are in a spiritually low place. I cannot judge your individual Iman and you cannot judge mine, but collectively, as a representation of average Muslims, we have a long way to climb. Our faith needs serious revival.

But here's the opportunity: If we're in this state and we now put ourselves to work—to fix ourselves, to build ourselves up, to spend our time, energy and resources for Allah's sake—we will be ranked by Allah ﷻ in a higher category than those who will come later and spend when victory is already established, when the infrastructure is already in place, when the hard work has been done.

Think of those early Muslims who came to America and couldn't find halal meat for years because they were working in remote areas. Two people pooled their life savings to build a small musalla, just so their children could maintain Islam. Those pioneers—they're in a different category than people who come later when a giant mosque is already built and now fundraising is for a gym addition. Both are good, but they're not in the same ranking.

### Everyone Has Been Promised Good

وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ (but to all Allah has promised the best). Don't misunderstand—just because you came later doesn't mean your contribution doesn't count. Everyone who spends and strives has been promised the very best from Allah ﷻ. No sincere effort goes to waste.

وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (and Allah is All-Aware of what you do). Allah ﷻ has complete, detailed knowledge of whatever you're doing. He knows your circumstances, your intentions, your struggles, your limitations. His awareness is comprehensive.

translation: Who is it that would loan Allah a goodly loan so He will multiply it for him and he will have a noble reward
commentary:
مَّن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يُقْرِضُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا فَيُضَـٰعِفَهُۥ لَهُۥ وَلَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ

**Who is it that would loan Allah a goodly loan so He may multiply it for him and he will have a noble reward?**

### The Profound Humility in Allah's Request

مَّن ذَا ٱلَّذِى (who is it that) is a call: Is there anyone? Anyone at all? يُقْرِضُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا (who would loan Allah a goodly loan).

When someone takes money and promises to repay it, they might say, "I'm good for it. I'm reliable. Give me a good loan." قَرْضًا حَسَنًا (a goodly loan) also implies there's no fixed deadline: "I'll pay you back whenever I can."

Now imagine this scenario: Your mother comes to you and says, "Can I borrow twenty dollars? I promise I'll pay you back when I can." Not "give me twenty dollars," but "may I borrow" with a promise to repay.

If my mother spoke to me this way, I would be devastated. My mother thinks I'm so greedy, so attached to money, that I wouldn't simply give her twenty dollars? That she has to ask to borrow it? Even worse, that she feels she must promise to pay me back because she's not sure I'd give it otherwise? This would be the most humiliating thing I could hear from my mother. She has stooped to this language because I've neglected her so much, because I've been so consumed with greed that she thinks the only way to convince me is to frame it as a loan?

How could I have let our relationship deteriorate to this point?

But we're not talking about your mother or mine. Allah ﷻ is asking this question. He could simply command: "Give Me what's Mine." After all, whose money is it anyway? What's in your wallet isn't truly yours. That debit card connects to an account, and the money in that account ultimately belongs to Allah ﷻ. He could demand it.

Instead, He uses this language: "Would anyone loan Me?" This expression isn't just beautiful—it's meant to shame us. How greedy have we become that Allah ﷻ must use loan terminology with us? This phrase should wound our integrity as Muslims, pierce our sense of honor. It's designed to make us say, "No, no, no, Allah! I give! I'm ready to give! It's Yours already!"

### The Multiplication and Noble Reward

فَيُضَـٰعِفَهُۥ لَهُۥ (so He may multiply it for him). Allah ﷻ doesn't just accept what you give—He multiplies it. The return is exponentially greater than what you invested.

وَلَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ (and he will have a noble reward). Beyond the multiplication, there's a gracious, dignified, noble reward waiting. The word كَرِيم  suggests generosity, honor and nobility. This isn't just any reward—it's a reward that elevates your status and dignity.

translation: On the Day you see the believing men and believing women, their light proceeding before them and on their right, [it will be said], "Your good tidings today are [of] gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein you will abide eternally." That is what is the great attainment
commentary:
يَوْمَ تَرَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ يَسْعَىٰ نُورُهُم بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَـٰنِهِم بُشْرَىٰكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

This ayah paints a vivid picture of the believers on Judgment Day. Imagine yourself walking step by step, and light is emanating from you—guiding your path forward. Then, as you walk with this divine light, an announcement comes: "Your good news today is gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein you will abide eternally." 

Consider the power of this moment. You've heard about Jannah your entire life. But now you're actually walking toward it on Judgment Day, and you haven't yet seen it with your own eyes. Then you hear that gardens with flowing rivers await you ahead. The walk toward Paradise feels endless, and you're told you will stay there forever. That eternal stay in the Fire would have been torment, but eternal stay in Paradise is described as الْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ —the ultimate, the greatest success.

This ayah establishes a critical foundation for understanding what follows: there are levels of Iman. There is intellectual Iman—knowing and accepting the truth mentally. And there is qualitative Iman—the condition of your heart, the depth of your conviction, the transformation of your character. If you don't take care of your qualitative Iman and only maintain intellectual Iman, that becomes weak Iman. And if you let your Iman stay weak long enough, it starts dipping down into hypocrisy. 

This is dangerous territory. Strong faith, weak faith, and then just one level below—hypocrisy. If you're in weak faith right now, you're only one step away from falling behind. You don't want to be there.

translation: On the [same] Day the hypocrite men and hypocrite women will say to those who believed, "Wait for us that we may acquire some of your light." It will be said, "Go back behind you and seek light." And a wall will be placed between them with a door, its interior containing mercy, but on the outside of it is torment
commentary:
يَوْمَ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتُ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱنظُرُونَا نَقْتَبِسْ مِن نُّورِكُمْ قِيلَ ٱرْجِعُوا۟ وَرَآءَكُمْ فَٱلْتَمِسُوا۟ نُورًا فَضُرِبَ بَيْنَهُم بِسُورٍ لَّهُۥ بَابٌۢ بَاطِنُهُۥ فِيهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةُ وَظَـٰهِرُهُۥ مِن قِبَلِهِ ٱلْعَذَابُ

Now let me tell you about the people of hypocrisy. This is the second phase of Judgment Day. The disbelievers have already been cast into Hellfire—they've been discarded. Now only believers and hypocrites remain, marching their way toward Paradise. The believers have light radiating from their chests, so they know where to go. They're moving quickly because they have both light and motivation.

But the hypocrites? They have no light. They didn't nurture their faith. Perhaps at some point they had intellectual faith, but even that was lost because of their weakness. They didn't take care of their hearts. There's no light emanating from their chests because the heart was void. When the heart is hard and empty, the deeds become meaningless—because deeds are nothing without good intentions rooted in the heart. No light in the heart means no light on Judgment Day.

Picture this: you're in darkness, walking, and some people around you have torches. You don't have one. What do you want to do? Stay close to those who have light so you can see by their glow. But if you get left behind and you realize you can't see them anymore, panic sets in.

The hypocrites call out: "انظُرُونَا" (—"Wait for us! Look at us!" Then they plead: "نَقْتَبِسْ مِن نُّورِكُمْ" —"Let us borrow some of your light!" The word اقْتِبَاس  means to light one torch from another, like borrowing a flame. Can we have some of your light so we can see?

Notice what happens next. Allah ﷻ doesn't even say that the believers respond to them. He says "قِيلَ" —"It will be said." An impersonal announcement is made, just as in previous ayahs where the speaker wasn't mentioned. This makes the rejection more devastating—it's not even worth a personal response.

The announcement declares: "ٱرْجِعُوا۟ وَرَآءَكُمْ فَٱلْتَمِسُوا۟ نُورًا" —"Go back behind you and seek light there. Get your own light. This light isn't for you."

These people are standing in complete darkness and they're told to turn around and go back. But they don't listen. They realize what turning back means, so they still try to catch up to the believers. 

Then Allah ﷻ says: "فَضُرِبَ بَيْنَهُم بِسُورٍ" —"A wall will be dropped between them." Not just any wall—a سُور , the massive walls that encircle entire cities. Not your backyard fence, but the kind of fortress wall you can't scale or breach.

"لَّهُۥ بَابٌۢ" —"It has a door." Why mention a door? The scholars explain that there are different levels of hypocrisy and weak faith. The Messenger ﷺ described people who will burn in Hellfire for a time but eventually make their way to Paradise. How will they cross if there's a giant wall? Through that door. But the door's mention also adds to their false hope—they see it, they try to open it, but it won't budge. It's insult added to injury.

"بَاطِنُهُۥ فِيهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةُ" —"Its interior contains mercy." On the side they cannot see, where the believers are, there is mercy. "وَظَـٰهِرُهُۥ مِن قِبَلِهِ ٱلْعَذَابُ" —"And its exterior, from their side, contains punishment."

translation: The hypocrites will call to the believers, "Were we not with you?" They will say, "Yes, but you afflicted yourselves and awaited [misfortune for us] and doubted, and wishful thinking deluded you until there came the command of Allah. And the Deceiver deceived you concerning Allah
commentary:
يُنَادُونَهُمْ أَلَمْ نَكُن مَّعَكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَلَـٰكِنَّكُمْ فَتَنتُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَتَرَبَّصْتُمْ وَٱرْتَبْتُمْ وَغَرَّتْكُمُ ٱلْأَمَانِىُّ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَغَرَّكُم بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْغَرُورُ

The walls don't have ceilings, so voices can still carry across. The hypocrites start calling out from their side: "أَلَمْ نَكُن مَّعَكُمْ" —"Weren't we with you? We were with you!" 

Imagine their desperate pleas: "We went to the same classes! Remember those Islamic seminars? You and I were inseparable! I used to go to the masjid too! I was a believer! I prayed in the front rows! I never missed Fajr! Remember when I got to the masjid before the adhan? I walked in before the mu'adhdhin started calling, and I kept walking all the way to the front!"

The believers respond: "بَلَىٰ" —"Yes, of course you were with us. We remember you."

But then comes the devastating diagnosis: "وَلَـٰكِنَّكُمْ فَتَنتُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ" —"But you put yourselves in tribulation." The word فِتْنَة  means a severe trial or test. You attacked yourselves. Notice—it doesn't say Shaytan put you in difficulty. You put yourself in difficulty. You were given the same guidance we received. You were given the same reminders we heard. But you didn't internalize any of it. You didn't translate it into heart transformation.

"وَتَرَبَّصْتُمْ" —"And you procrastinated. You waited and said, 'We'll see. We've got time. I'll change eventually. I know I need to make changes in myself, but it can wait.'"

One of my teachers, a very wise man, once described the struggle for our religion in a way that has never left me. He said: You have a seed. You must water it, give it sunlight, keep the soil soft, protect it from weeds. You take care of that seed so the plant can grow. But growing the plant isn't the ultimate goal. The goal is that the plant grows into a tree, and that tree bears fruit, and the fruit benefits others.

We have to take care of ourselves spiritually. I need to make an honest list of things I don't like about myself as a Muslim. There are problems I have, things I wish were different about me. I have to plant that seed and water it and care for myself. When I develop to a certain point, only then will I truly be able to benefit anyone else.

Our religion wants us to benefit others, but you can't benefit others while you're still an undeveloped seed. You have to mature first. But here's the trap: some people spend their entire lives just watering and raking, watering and raking. When you ask them about benefiting others, they say, "No, no, I still have more watering and raking to do." That's false spirituality.

Our faith is not only about ourselves. Yes, we will work on ourselves our entire lives. But while we're working on ourselves, there will come a point where we must honestly ask: Is there something I can benefit someone else with? If so, do it. You have to step up. You can't say, "Someone else out there can do it." You must take that step while never forgetting your own spiritual needs.

These hypocrites had no sense of urgency. "وَٱرْتَبْتُمْ" —"And you fell into doubt." We're learning something profound here: when you don't move forward on the path of Allah ﷻ, your qualitative Iman weakens. And when it weakens to a critical point, it starts affecting your intellectual Iman. You begin having doubts about Islam altogether. This happens to so many people today—they're in that state because something qualitative is missing, and it leads them to question the very foundations of their faith.

"وَغَرَّتْكُمُ ٱلْأَمَانِىُّ" —"And false hopes deluded you." What are these false hopes? "Allah ﷻ won't throw everyone in Hell. There are so many people worse than me who deserve punishment more. Why would He come after little old me for my little laziness? It's not that big a deal. I'll make it up eventually." Wishful thinking.

Or perhaps your hopes became entirely worldly—from one promotion to the next, one investment to the next, one workout routine to the next. These became your only aspirations. You forgot about the Hereafter entirely.

"حَتَّىٰ جَآءَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ" —"Until the decree of Allah ﷻ came." Death arrived. "وَغَرَّكُم بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْغَرُورُ"—"And the ultimate deceiver deceived you about Allah ﷻ." Shaytan got the best of you.

translation: So today no ransom will be taken from you or from those who disbelieved. Your refuge is the Fire. It is most worthy of you, and wretched is the destination
commentary:
فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ وَلَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ مَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ هِىَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

"فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ" —"So today, no ransom will be accepted from you." You cannot buy your way out. "وَلَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟" —"Nor from those who disbelieved."

Notice how they've been grouped together now. The hypocrites and the open disbelievers—same destination.

"مَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ" —"Your refuge is the Fire." "هِىَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ" —"It is your protector now. It is the one that will guard you now." The bitter irony—the Fire becomes their mawla, their guardian. "وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ" —"And what a horrible final destination."

translation: Has the time not come for those who have believed that their hearts should become humbly submissive at the remembrance of Allah and what has come down of the truth? And let them not be like those who were given the Scripture before, and a long period passed over them, so their hearts hardened; and many of them are defiantly disobedient
commentary:
۞ أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا نَزَلَ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا يَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلُ فَطَالَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَمَدُ فَقَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ

This is one of the most powerful ayahs of divine complaint to Muslims in the entire Quran. It cuts deep because it's so direct, so personal.

"أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟" —"Has not the time come for the believers?" The word يَأْنِ  means "hasn't it arrived yet?" Hasn't the moment come? Hasn't it been long enough?

"أَن تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ" —"That their hearts should be humbled by the remembrance of Allah ﷻ?" The word خُشُوع  means hearts melting, being overwhelmed, filled with awe. This is qualitative Iman—something happening in the heart.

"وَمَا نَزَلَ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ" —"And by what has been revealed of the truth"—meaning the Quran. Isn't it time yet that the Quran started having a real impact on you? That your heart trembles when you hear it? That you're moved when you recite it?

Then comes the warning: "وَلَا يَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلُ" )—"And let them not be like those who were given the Scripture before." Allah ﷻ is warning us not to become like previous nations who received revelation.

"فَطَالَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَمَدُ" —"And a long period passed over them." Generation after generation. "فَقَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ" —"So their hearts became hardened."

They were given books too. They recited those books too. Their tongues moved with the words, their minds memorized the verses, but their hearts? Their hearts were empty. Their hearts became hard. What happens to a building when you don't maintain it? It deteriorates. It becomes an empty shell, a ruin. That's what their hearts became—ruins of faith.

When did we reach that state? How many people only call the masjid during Ramadan? The phone never rings except when Ramadan comes. On the 27th night, the phones go off the hook: "Which night are we supposed to worship? What time should we pray? When should we try to get close to Allah ﷻ?"

For so many Muslims, that's the only night they might pray a little extra. That's how deteriorated the relationship has become. What a magnificent message was revealed, and what we've reduced it to—a few practices, a few rituals. That's all our Islam has become.

"وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ" —"And many of them are defiantly disobedient." When Allah ﷻ says a long period passed over the previous nations and their hearts became hard, we must ask ourselves: hasn't a millennium and a half passed over this Ummah? Are we not in danger of our hearts becoming hard?

translation: Know that Allah gives life to the earth after its lifelessness. We have made clear to you the signs; perhaps you will understand
commentary:
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۚ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ

But Allah ﷻ, being the ultimate Teacher, doesn't leave us in despair. After that devastating warning, He gives us hope and motivation.

"ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا" —"Know that Allah ﷻ gives life to the earth after its death."

Allah ﷻ is comparing your heart and mine to dead earth. How does He give life to the earth? He sends water from the sky. How does He give life to our hearts? He sends Quran from the sky.

This ayah should transform how we view the Quran. The love we're supposed to have for memorizing Allah's ﷻ words—I cannot fully express it. The love we're supposed to have for reciting Quran slowly, calmly, reflecting on what we recite. How grateful we should feel when we memorize even one ayah. 

This is a treasure for which there is no equivalent on earth. The words of Allah ﷻ that came from the highest heaven are now sitting inside your chest. They were in a hidden, protected vault in the seventh heaven—مَكْنُون —and now they're in this vault, your heart. That's a gift from Allah ﷻ. You've just been connected to Him by His own words.

When I first started learning even a little bit of Arabic, just enough to catch every other word, I was overwhelmed. You stumble upon a treasure you have no right to access, you're not qualified, you're not worthy to be in this vault, and you get a glimpse inside. You and I should become overwhelmed with gratitude.

How many people don't even feel the need? They don't even know this treasure exists. May Allah ﷻ grant us access to it.

"قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ" —"We have made clear to you the signs so that you may understand."

The next time you see rain, the next time you see plants emerging from dead earth, remember what should happen to our Iman. Remember that rain came for the dead earth, and Quran came for our dead hearts.

translation: Indeed, the men who practice charity and the women who practice charity and [they who] have loaned Allah a goodly loan - it will be multiplied for them, and they will have a noble reward
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلْمُصَّدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُصَّدِّقَـٰتِ وَأَقْرَضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا يُضَـٰعَفُ لَهُمْ وَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ

"إِنَّ ٱلْمُصَّدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُصَّدِّقَـٰتِ" —"Indeed, the men who give in charity and the women who give in charity." These are people who confirm the truth of their faith through their actions.

"وَأَقْرَضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا" —"And who lend to Allah ﷻ a goodly loan." They give and give and give, proving the sincerity of their faith through their generosity.

"يُضَـٰعَفُ لَهُمْ" —"It will be multiplied for them." "وَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ" —"And they will have a noble, generous compensation."

translation: And those who have believed in Allah and His messengers - those are [in the ranks of] the supporters of truth and the martyrs, with their Lord. For them is their reward and their light. But those who have disbelieved and denied Our verses - those are the companions of Hellfire
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصِّدِّيقُونَ ۖ وَٱلشُّهَدَآءُ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

"وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصِّدِّيقُونَ" —"And those who believed in Allah ﷻ and His messengers—those are the ones who are truly the siddiqun."

Not just صَادِق (truthful), but صِدِّيق —the intensive form. This is an extremely rare and powerful form in Arabic. These are the people of ultimate truthfulness, the highest level of confirming truth through both belief and action.

Understand that whenever Iman is discussed in this surah, it's not merely about becoming Muslim in a superficial sense. Many translations don't distinguish between intellectual Iman and qualitative Iman. You must understand this from the context of the entire surah.

"وَٱلشُّهَدَآءُ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ" —"And the martyrs are with their Lord." "لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ" —"They will have their reward and their light."

This brings us full circle back to the beginning—that light which illuminates the path to Paradise.

"وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ" —"But those who disbelieved and denied Our signs—those are the companions of Hellfire."

The contrast is complete. Light and darkness. Truth and falsehood. Paradise and Hellfire. The choice, ultimately, is about what we do with our hearts today.

translation: Know that the life of this world is but amusement and diversion and adornment and boasting to one another and competition in increase of wealth and children - like the example of a rain whose [resulting] plant growth pleases the tillers; then it dries and you see it turned yellow; then it becomes [scattered] debris. And in the Hereafter is severe punishment and forgiveness from Allah and approval. And what is the worldly life except the enjoyment of delusion
commentary:
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ وَزِينَةٌ وَتَفَاخُرٌۢ بَيْنَكُمْ وَتَكَاثُرٌ فِى ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَوْلَـٰدِ ۖ كَمَثَلِ غَيْثٍ أَعْجَبَ ٱلْكُفَّارَ نَبَاتُهُۥ ثُمَّ يَهِيجُ فَتَرَىٰهُ مُصْفَرًّا ثُمَّ يَكُونُ حُطَـٰمًا ۖ وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ ۚ وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْغُرُورِ

If iman is all about using our time wisely and setting the right priorities, then what are the competing priorities that distract us from the real priorities of faith? Allah ﷻ gives us a comprehensive answer in this remarkable ayah.

ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ - you had better realize, you had better know, you had better understand. أَنَّمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا - that worldly life, the lower life, the lesser life, the lowest life - ad-dunya comes from dana, meaning low. This life amounts to nothing more than what follows.

لَعِبٌ - playing, games. When a child is young, what's the most important thing to them? Playing. Can I have that toy? Can we go to the park? Can we play hide and seek? A child will skip food, forget everything else, just to keep playing. They're in the zone. Nothing else matters.

وَلَهْوٌ - entertainment, distraction. You get a little older and discover there are shows, movies, stories, songs, endless forms of entertainment. A new virus gets installed on your system, and suddenly entertainment becomes essential. You cannot live without it.

وَزِينَةٌ  - beautification, adornment. Then you become self-conscious about your image. How do I look? What should I wear? What brand, what color? You're trying to match everything perfectly. People become obsessed with their appearance - their hair, their clothing, their accessories. Your image becomes everything to you. The beautification extends beyond yourself to your house, your car, everything around you must look a certain way.

وَتَفَاخُرٌۢ بَيْنَكُمْ  - mutual boasting, showing off to each other. The image concern evolves into competition. Which college did you get into? Where did you graduate from? You want to make sure others know what you've accomplished. Even two people in the most humble professions can compete over the smallest things. You get obsessed with your label, your title, your achievements. 

As you transition into adulthood, you're out of your parents' house with your own apartment, your own car. You have to get the latest phone, the newest model. "Look at this! So much better, right? Can't you tell?" Even when there's barely any difference, being first to have it matters.

وَتَكَاثُرٌ فِى ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَوْلَـٰدِ - competition in accumulating wealth and children. Eventually, even the showing off fades, and you move past it. What remains is the constant focus on getting more and more. Your mind becomes consumed with shared concerns about your children and money - savings, expenses, bills, mortgage payments, insurance, college accounts, retirement funds. Ask any parent at any given time, whether they're conscious of it or not, what's running through their head? Kids and money. Children and finances. Got to pick them up from school, buy these books, pay for that, handle this, manage that. It constantly invades your mind and takes you over.

These are the distractions of life. This is what your worldly existence amounts to. 

Then Allah ﷻ provides a powerful parable: كَمَثَلِ غَيْثٍ  - like the example of perfect rain, rain that comes at exactly the right moment, rain you were hoping for. The farmer - described as كُفَّار , meaning those who bury seeds in the ground - is impressed by the produce that results from that rain.

Consider the farmer's situation. When he buries a seed in the ground, he has no idea how the crop will turn out. No clue. He doesn't know if it will rain, if it will be a good year or a bad year. He must do all this work without knowing any results. But when one day he wakes up and sees rainfall, when he starts seeing little sprouts of green, he's filled with hope. He's ecstatic. Agricultural societies literally have festivals celebrating rain because they know it means the entire year will be good. 

Imagine being stressed about getting a paycheck on Friday. The farmer gets a paycheck once a year - one annual paycheck. If it doesn't rain, that year's income isn't coming. So when it rains, he knows his harvest is around the corner. He's overjoyed.

Why does Allah ﷻ compare all those worldly pursuits to rain and a farmer? Because of what happens next in the life cycle of both the crop and our worldly attachments.

The child desperately wants that toy. From a distance, he can see it in the store. His eyes light up - أَعْجَبَ - it impresses him completely. This is actually happening! You get called for that second interview and your eyes bulge out. The loan application goes through and you're about to move into your house. You're in your last semester and getting butterflies because graduation is near. These are milestones people celebrate with intense emotion.

ثُمَّ يَهِيجُ فَتَرَىٰهُ مُصْفَرًّا  - then it matures and you watch it turn yellow. This is profound. If a crop is meant to be harvested, it should be cut before it turns yellow. When it turns yellow, it means the farmer wasn't interested in cutting it. He didn't seize the opportunity. He lost interest.

This is exactly what happens with worldly life. You get something, then you lose interest in it. You wanted that toy more than anything in the world - until there's another toy you don't have. You wanted that car desperately - until next year's model came out. Now you wonder why you even wasted money on this one. You loved your house more than anything - until you visited someone else's house and thought, "Did you see their house? That was amazing!" You just become dissatisfied. It turns yellow for you. The excitement dies.

ثُمَّ يَكُونُ حُطَـٰمًا  - then it becomes something you just step on, something worthless. You don't even notice it anymore. That money, those material things, the beautification - all of it becomes worthless. The value completely disappears.

وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ  - And in the Hereafter, there is either intense punishment or forgiveness from Allah ﷻ and His pleasure, His contentment. The word رِضْوَٰنٌ  also means you'll have pleasure, you'll be content. You'll finally find the one thing you cannot find in anything else: true contentment, genuine satisfaction.

Allah ﷻ is telling us those worldly things will be there. They're part of life. But don't let them distract you from the fundamental reality: you and I are here to either save ourselves from punishment or earn Allah's forgiveness and truly find satisfaction where satisfaction can actually be found.

وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْغُرُورِ  - And what is worldly life except means by which people get deceived? Mata' means provisions, tools, means. Al-ghurur means deception. This life is nothing but means of deception, tools by which we deceive ourselves into thinking this is where meaning lies, this is where satisfaction can be found, this is what truly matters. 

The ayah strips away that deception and shows us the reality: these pursuits are temporary, unsatisfying, and ultimately empty. Real contentment, lasting reward, and true success are found only in the Hereafter, earned through iman and righteous action in this fleeting life.

translation: Race toward forgiveness from your Lord and a Garden whose width is like the width of the heavens and earth, prepared for those who believed in Allah and His messengers. That is the bounty of Allah which He gives to whom He wills, and Allah is the possessor of great bounty
commentary:
سَابِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا كَعَرْضِ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أُعِدَّتْ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ

In the previous ayah, Allah ﷻ described what keeps human beings busy in worldly life. When that life isn't working toward forgiveness and saving ourselves, it becomes nothing more than a means by which people are deceived. Now in ayah 21, Allah ﷻ redirects us: while you've been running toward those things, here's what you really should have been racing toward.

The concept of  (السابقون) - those who race to be first - connects directly to themes from Surah Al-Waqi'ah. There we heard about the people of Sabiq, the first and the foremost. Now Allah ﷻ commands us to engage in that race ourselves: سَابِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ - "Race toward forgiveness from your Master."

This is not a casual stroll toward salvation. The verb سَابِقُوا conveys urgency, competition, striving to outpace others. Race toward forgiveness from your Lord, وَجَنَّةٍ - and race toward a Garden. If you want to race toward something truly valuable, something that won't become pale and boring after a while, something that won't lose its luster, then race toward Allah's forgiveness and His Jannah.

The description of this Garden is stunning: عَرْضُهَا كَعَرْضِ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ - "its expanse is like the expanse of the sky and the earth." The word عَرْض  refers to width, stretch, or expanse. Notice Allah ﷻ uses السَّمَآءِ  - not just the seven heavens, but sama in its unlimited sense. Everything above you is sama. This isn't referring to a limited, defined space. The word sama here is unlimited, suggesting an infinite expanse.

This Garden with its infinite reach أُعِدَّتْ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ - has been prepared for those who truly believed in Allah ﷻ and His messengers. Not a superficial belief, but deep, qualitative faith.

Then comes a critical statement: ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ - "That is the favor of Allah. He grants it to whoever He wills." This phrase pushes us to think beyond a minimalist view of Islam. There's a perspective that says: here are the five pillars, stay away from major sins, fulfill your major obligations, and you're set for Jannah. That's a quantitative, checklist approach to faith.

But this surah emphasizes qualitative Iman. The qualitative view suggests that those five pillars and avoiding major sins is just the starting point - the bare minimum that keeps you from being considered a criminal. Not being a criminal isn't an accomplishment; it just means you're a law-abiding citizen of Islam.

Islam demands something more - some kind of struggle, some kind of service. Islam is greater than your personal adherence. It should become a reality on earth. People should know about it. It should bring light and justice to business dealings, social interactions, families, neighborhoods and communities. Islam isn't only about fixing your relationship with Allah ﷻ and making your personal life better. It addresses the problems of society.

If each of us is only concerned about our personal Islam, then we're serving our individual religious needs, but we're not serving the religion itself and its higher goals in society. Look at the struggles of the prophets throughout the Quran. Yes, they called people to believe in Allah ﷻ, but they also confronted real societal problems. Shu'ayb (AS) criticized corrupt business practices. Lut (AS) addressed rampant immorality. Isa (AS) exposed religious corruption and the commercialization of faith. Musa (AS) stood against the enslavement of an entire race.

These messengers weren't just dealing with abstract theological concepts. They engaged with actual wrongs happening on the ground. They understood that their faith didn't just reform them personally - it reformed families, communities and entire societies. Islam makes us concerned about injustices wherever they occur.

There are different views on how this should manifest. Some say if a few people in society are doing this work - whether you call it dawah, education or awareness - then others are fine just maintaining their individual obligations. My personal view, shaped by exposure to various Islamic movements and traditional scholarship, is still forming. But one thing is clear: Islam is more than just about ourselves.

At minimum, Islam requires concern for more than just myself. The Prophet ﷺ emphasized care for neighbors as fundamental to faith. Simple hadith about removing harm from the path show concern for others from the very essence of Islam. This guidance was revealed not only to better our lives here and remove oppression, but to save people from eternal punishment. If we're truly humanitarian, truly concerned about human beings, we must be concerned about their hereafter.

This is a struggle, and each of us must figure out where we fit in it. I no longer believe Islam is one single cause, one project, one movement. There are multiple legitimate causes, and as long as Muslims find themselves talented enough to give themselves to one of those causes, they're doing what they should to keep their Iman alive and growing. Because now they're benefiting others, not just themselves. That's true spirituality - seeing yourself as part of Islam's mission in some way.

Children's education is a cause. Women's education is a cause. Dawah to specific communities is a cause. These are specific efforts that need struggle and dedication. The classical flaw of movements was seeing themselves as the only legitimate movement, undermining other efforts. But if you take a step back, there are enough legitimate causes within our religion that need service.

When we talk about racing in this ayah, we're not just talking about racing to become a better Muslim through personal obligations. We're talking about something beyond that. And when you struggle in Allah's cause, when you race toward this goal, you're going to face problems. This world attacks your motivation from two sides: distractions and pleasures on one side, and problems and difficulties on the other.

translation: No disaster strikes upon the earth or among yourselves except that it is in a register before We bring it into being - indeed that, for Allah, is easy
commentary:
مَآ أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّبْرَأَهَآ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ

On one side of the command to race toward forgiveness were temptations and distractions. On the other side are problems and calamities. This ayah addresses those difficulties directly.

مَآ أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ - "Not a single targeted calamity." The word مُصِيبَة  is carefully chosen. In Arabic, it's used for an arrow that hits its target precisely. It's also used for calamity. By using this word for difficulties, Allah ﷻ lets us know that whatever problem occurred was exactly on target - sniper fire from Him. This is precisely the problem you were supposed to have.

فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ - "In the earth or deep inside yourselves." Any problem that occurs anywhere in the earth, or any struggle that happens in your personal life, إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّبْرَأَهَآ - it's in a book from before We created it.

The verb نَّبْرَأَ  is significant. One meaning of the root برأ is to create, but interestingly, البُرء also means to be relieved, to be healthy after being sick. So Allah ﷻ calls the problems that come our way in the struggle for Islam a kind of relief. Relief from what? Perhaps relief from the mistakes we've made in our cause. Allah ﷻ hits us with a problem, we struggle through it, and that struggle becomes an expiation for our errors. He relieves us of the flaws we had in our efforts.

Or perhaps these challenges are relief in another sense - they're a means by which our ranks are raised even higher. We all have potential that won't emerge until we push ourselves to the limit. Allah ﷻ gives those who can handle it even more problems so they can overcome them, and their ranks with Him will be elevated.

إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ - "Indeed, that is easy for Allah." All of this - recording everything before it happens, targeting each problem precisely, turning difficulties into relief - is effortless for Him.

translation: In order that you not despair over what has eluded you and not exult [in pride] over what He has given you. And Allah does not like everyone self-deluded and boastful
commentary:
لِّكَيْلَا تَأْسَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَاتَكُمْ وَلَا تَفْرَحُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ

Allah ﷻ does all of this to develop a strong personality in us. The way He develops this strength is captured beautifully in this ayah.

لِّكَيْلَا تَأْسَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَاتَكُمْ - "So that you do not grieve over what you lose." When you're struggling for Allah ﷻ - trying to build a masjid, establish a school, serve in any capacity - problems happen. Someone dies, permits get denied, funding falls through. Allah ﷻ says when you truly struggle for Him, you stop becoming devastated by losses. You're not sad about setbacks because you're worried about something deeper than the project itself.

What happens to people when they work for Islam is their project can become their Islam. That project itself becomes Islam in their mind. But the project is not Islam - it's just a means to serve Islam. When that project fails, stops or gets shut down, your Islam doesn't stop. You turn to Allah ﷻ and say, "That project is gone, but I'm still here. My passion to serve You is still burning. I'll move on to the next thing." Your loyalty isn't to a cause or a project. Your loyalty is to the service of Islam itself.

The Sahaba (RA) were listening to this message in a specific context. Their "projects" were Badr and Uhud - the battles themselves. When they lost seventy shaheed at Uhud, Allah ﷻ was essentially saying: I did this to you so you stop grieving about things you lose in My path.

وَلَا تَفْرَحُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ - "And so you do not become overly joyful over what you gain." The flip side is equally important. Don't become excessively happy over your successes. Just because you achieved success in your cause doesn't mean you're automatically successful with Allah ﷻ. That success could also be a test.

Just as you shouldn't become overly depressed at failure, you shouldn't become overly excited at success. Both reactions make you lose sight of the reality: you're still in the middle of the exam. You're still being tested. You're still in the heart of the struggle. Allah ﷻ gives you problems and successes to keep you from becoming people hung up on either failures or victories. Rather, you stay focused on the mission itself.

وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ - "And Allah does not love any person who is mukhtal and fakhur." The word مُخْتَالٍ  comes from خَيَال , meaning imagination. A mukhtal is someone with an imaginary, inflated sense of self. They have a powerful, deluded self-image: "Because of me, this struggle succeeded. Because of me, this happened and that happened."

Usually this expression describes materialistic, arrogant, self-absorbed people. But here the context is about struggling for Allah ﷻ. Even in righteous work, this spiritual disease can creep in. Even in serving Islam, you can develop this inflated ego, this sense that your contribution is what made the difference.

This is why Allah ﷻ structures the struggle with both difficulties and successes - to keep you humble, to keep you real, to keep you aware that everything is from Him, and you're simply honored to be a servant in His cause.

translation: [Those] who are stingy and enjoin upon people stinginess. And whoever turns away - then indeed, Allah is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَيَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبُخْلِ ۗ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ

This ayah describes those who don't struggle in Allah's path and don't spend, contrasting with the beginning of the surah which emphasized spending. When wealth becomes a source of pride, you naturally become reluctant to part with it because giving it away would diminish the very thing that gives you status and position.

The phrase الَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَيَأْمُرُونَ النَّاسَ بِالْبُخْلِ (those who are miserly and command people to be miserly) appears several times in the Quran. Why do they not only hoard wealth themselves but also encourage others to do the same? The munafiq (hypocrite) finds himself in an uncomfortable position: the Messenger ﷺ calls people to spend in Allah's path, everyone responds, but he alone refuses. He stands out like a black sheep. Not wanting to be exposed, he devises an ingenious plan—he tells others, "I know he says to spend, but you don't have to go crazy. You have a family and finances to be responsible for. I'm not being cheap, I'm just being responsible." By encouraging others to be "responsible," he masks his own miserliness.

The ayah concludes with a powerful reminder: whoever turns away, Allah ﷻ is الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ —the Ultimately Independent, the Ultimately Praised. Allah ﷻ doesn't need your money or your praise. If you choose to be cheap, go ahead—He is already free of all need and already praiseworthy.

translation: We have already sent Our messengers with clear evidences and sent down with them the Scripture and the balance that the people may maintain [their affairs] in justice. And We sent down iron, wherein is great military might and benefits for the people, and so that Allah may make evident those who support Him and His messengers unseen. Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might
commentary:
لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَأَنزَلْنَا مَعَهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ لِيَقُومَ ٱلنَّاسُ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَأَنزَلْنَا ٱلْحَدِيدَ فِيهِ بَأْسٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَنَـٰفِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُۥ وَرُسُلَهُۥ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهُ قَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ

This is one of the most explicit ayahs in the Quran regarding the mission of all prophets, and particularly the mission of our Messenger ﷺ. Allah ﷻ sent His messengers with clear proofs (بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ), and with them He sent down the Book (الْكِتَابَ) and the Scale (الْمِيزَانَ). The Book represents religious teaching and doctrine, while the Scale represents the principles of justice. The scale is the universal, historical symbol of justice found in courtrooms across civilizations.

The purpose of both was لِيَقُومَ النَّاسُ بِالْقِسْطِ (so that people could stand upright with justice). This teaches us that individuals in any society cannot be truly fair until they hold onto the Book, and when individuals hold onto the Book, society will hold onto the Scale—meaning proper justice will prevail.

Then comes an extraordinary statement: وَأَنزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ (and We sent down iron). Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ: You haven't just been given a Book and justice—I've also sent down iron. What does this mean? Iron contains بَأْسٌ شَدِيدٌ (intense means of war)—it's used to make swords and shields for battle. To establish justice in Arabia, war was also necessary. This metal is a gift from Allah ﷻ for building the instruments needed to defend and establish justice, because without this, humanity would never have a living model of justice. The Quran would remain merely theoretical, like Plato's Republic—beautiful ideas never witnessed in reality.

But the ayah continues: وَمَنَافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ (and benefits for people). Once war is over, metal shouldn't only serve military purposes—it has countless peaceful applications. This teaches us a crucial sociological principle: war machines should be built in times of war, but when conflict ends, those resources must shift to benefit society. 

When a society becomes obsessed with building war machines regardless of whether war exists, the industries producing them stockpile weapons and seek to exhaust them through continuous warfare to justify new contracts. The war-industrial complex perpetuates conflict to sustain itself. This remains tragically relevant today, where the wealthiest companies profit from warfare and political platforms remain intertwined with military spending to sustain economies built on weapon manufacturing.

The ayah concludes: وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُ وَرُسُلَهُ بِالْغَيْبِ (and so that Allah may make evident who aids Him and His messengers in the unseen). When the Sahaba stood beside the Prophet ﷺ in battle against the disbelievers of Makkah, they proved their loyalty not just to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, but to all messengers throughout history. They demonstrated belief in the unseen by supporting a prophetic mission they couldn't see with their eyes but accepted with their hearts.

إِنَّ اللَّهَ قَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ (Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might). Even though the ayah mentions the mechanisms of power—war, iron, strength, muscle—at the end of the day, we have no power. Allah ﷻ is the Ultimate Power.

translation: And We have already sent Noah and Abraham and placed in their descendants prophethood and scripture; and among them is he who is guided, but many of them are defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا وَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِى ذُرِّيَّتِهِمَا ٱلنُّبُوَّةَ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مُّهْتَدٍ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ

After establishing that believers see themselves as part of the legacy of all previous prophets, Allah ﷻ mentions the two greatest messengers before Muhammad ﷺ who were closest to being universal messengers to all humanity: Nuh (AS) and Ibrahim (AS).

Nuh (AS) could be considered a messenger to all humanity because after the flood, no other people remained—everyone descended from those who survived with him. Ibrahim (AS) holds this distinction because all prophets after him came from his lineage. While Nuh's (AS) descendants included other prophets, and the Semitic peoples came from his son Sam, it was specifically from Ibrahim's (AS) line that prophethood continued uninterrupted until our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

Allah ﷻ placed in their offspring النُّبُوَّةَ وَالْكِتَابَ (prophethood and the Book). Yet the reality is sobering: فَمِنْهُم مُّهْتَدٍ (among them are those committed to guidance), وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَاسِقُونَ (but a multitude of them are corrupt). Despite being descendants of these noble prophets, many strayed from the path.

translation: Then We sent following their footsteps Our messengers and followed [them] with Jesus, the son of Mary, and gave him the Gospel. And We placed in the hearts of those who followed him compassion and mercy and monasticism, which they innovated; We did not prescribe it for them except [that they did so] seeking the approval of Allah. But they did not observe it with due observance. So We gave the ones who believed among them their reward, but many of them are defiantly disobedient
commentary:
ثُمَّ قَفَّيْنَا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم بِرُسُلِنَا وَقَفَّيْنَا بِعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْإِنجِيلَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُ رَأْفَةً وَرَحْمَةً وَرَهْبَانِيَّةً ٱبْتَدَعُوهَا مَا كَتَبْنَـٰهَا عَلَيْهِمْ إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ رِضْوَٰنِ ٱللَّهِ فَمَا رَعَوْهَا حَقَّ رِعَايَتِهَا ۖ فَـَٔاتَيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ

The word قَفَّيْنَا means to follow right behind someone's footsteps closely. Allah ﷻ followed up the prophets from Ibrahim's (AS) lineage with successive messengers, culminating with Isa ibn Maryam (AS), the last major messenger before Muhammad ﷺ. Allah ﷻ gave him the Injeel (Gospel) and placed in the hearts of his true followers رَأْفَةً وَرَحْمَةً (compassion and mercy).

Then comes a critical observation: وَرَهْبَانِيَّةً ابْتَدَعُوهَا (and monasticism—they invented it). The concept of رَهْبَانِيَّة—leaving the world, wearing uncomfortable clothes, becoming monks isolated from society—was something they created entirely on their own. This surah addresses the comprehensive nature of true spirituality, and this ayah warns against one extreme.

Real spirituality is what the Sahaba embodied: people of great iman combined with great struggle for Islam. They balanced two essential elements—concern for their own spiritual development and active service beyond themselves. For our iman to survive and thrive, we must maintain both internal spiritual work and external activism for this deen.
