When this balance disappears, two problems emerge. The first was mentioned earlier in the surah: hearts becoming hard—intellectualism without spirituality, Islam without heart. The second problem is the opposite: pursuing only the spiritual, "feel-good" side without real-world engagement. This is what some Christians developed—a worldview where as long as you can cry in supplication, pray, and feel closeness to God, you're done. They invented the extreme form of spirituality: monasticism.

مَا كَتَبْنَاهَا عَلَيْهِمْ (We did not prescribe it for them). Allah ﷻ explicitly states this wasn't His command. The fact that this warning appears in the Quran means this danger exists for Muslims too. We can fall into either extreme: becoming hard-hearted intellectuals for whom Islam is completely heartless scholarship, or becoming so "spiritual" that we refuse to engage with the world. The latter manifests as someone who says, "Who am I to speak? Who am I to advise my father not to work at a liquor store? I'm just going to make istighfar and eat the halal chicken he bought with haram money." This is false spirituality, feel-good spirituality that doesn't translate into any goodness in the world.

There's a famous hadith where Allah ﷻ commands His angels to destroy a village. The angels protest: "But there's someone there who hasn't stopped worshiping You for the blink of an eye!" Allah ﷻ responds: "Start with him." The most spiritual person? Yes, start with him—because his blood didn't boil when Allah's commands were being trampled in the streets. The disobedience to Allah ﷻ happening around him didn't bother him at all.

If you return from Hajj with only a spiritual experience and nothing bothers you about what you witnessed, something is wrong. You should see problems, feel disturbed by injustice, be moved to action—otherwise you're like that monk, disconnected from the world's reality.

The Christians who invented monasticism had good intentions: إِلَّا ابْتِغَاءَ رِضْوَانِ اللَّهِ (except seeking Allah's pleasure). But فَمَا رَعَوْهَا حَقَّ رِعَايَتِهَا (they did not observe it with due observance)—they couldn't maintain it. When you go beyond what Allah ﷻ prescribed, attempting to be more spiritual than He commanded, you won't be able to sustain it.

The history of the church illustrates this painfully. When natural human desires are completely blocked rather than channeled Islamically, something twisted inevitably emerges. Allah ﷻ placed desires within us and provided halal means to fulfill them in healthy ways. When those outlets are denied entirely, the dam will break—just as water blocked by a dam without alternative channels will burst through in destructive ways. The scandals that have emerged from institutions promoting extreme abstinence bear witness to this reality.

فَآتَيْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ (So We gave those among them who truly believed their reward), وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَاسِقُونَ (but many of them are corrupt). Even among those who claimed to follow this path, only a few genuinely maintained their faith while many became corrupt.

translation: O you who have believed, fear Allah and believe in His Messenger; He will [then] give you a double portion of His mercy and make for you a light by which you will walk and forgive you; and Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَءَامِنُوا۟ بِرَسُولِهِۦ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ نُورًا تَمْشُونَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

After describing those who deviated into monasticism, Allah ﷻ now addresses the believers directly: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ (O you who have believed, be conscious of Allah and believe in His Messenger).

The reward for this balanced approach is threefold:

**يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِن رَّحْمَتِهِ** (He will give you twice the portion from His mercy)—a double compensation for maintaining the balance between spirituality and action.

**وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ نُورًا تَمْشُونَ بِهِ** (And He will place for you a light by which you will walk)—guidance that illuminates your path through life's complexities.

**وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ** (And He will forgive you)—covering your sins and shortcomings.

وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful). These final divine names remind us that despite our struggles and imperfections, His mercy and forgiveness remain ever-present.

translation: [This is] so that the People of the Scripture may know that they are not able [to obtain] anything from the bounty of Allah and that [all] bounty is in the hand of Allah; He gives it to whom He wills. And Allah is the possessor of great bounty
commentary:
لِّئَلَّا يَعْلَمَ أَهْلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَلَّا يَقْدِرُونَ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ مِّن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ ۙ وَأَنَّ ٱلْفَضْلَ بِيَدِ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ

This concluding ayah can be understood in two ways. Some translations interpret it negatively: "So that the People of the Book may know they have no control over Allah's favor," implying Muslims have replaced them as recipients of divine favor, and their only path to that favor now is through Islam.

However, there is a more positive interpretation that better fits the context. Respecting the particle لِئَلَّا  in its meaning of "so that not," we can understand it as: "So that the People of the Book don't misunderstand and think they have no portion of Allah's favor remaining." In other words, this message brings them hope—it shows them that a way forward still exists. The final message has arrived, and with it comes the opportunity for them to access Allah's favor once again.

This interpretation connects beautifully with the entire purpose of the surah. Allah ﷻ wanted to soften hearts, and He described those who softened their hearts with good intentions through monasticism but couldn't sustain it. Yet among them, a few did maintain sincerity. There remain People of the Book who are genuinely searching for balance, seeking an authentic spiritual lifestyle that they cannot find in religions that have undergone changes and distortions.

The surah tells believers: وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ (believe in His Messenger) and live by his guidance, so that the People of the Book can witness this balance. When they see the beauty of Islam lived authentically—the harmony between deep spirituality and meaningful action—they will know that Allah's favor has continued, that it now resides with the Muslim Ummah. Witnessing this living example, they can find their way to that balance once again.

وَأَنَّ الْفَضْلَ بِيَدِ اللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَاءُ (And that all favor is in the hand of Allah; He gives it to whom He wills). The ultimate message is that Allah's favor isn't restricted or monopolized—it remains entirely in His control, granted according to His wisdom.

وَاللَّهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ (And Allah is the Possessor of Great Bounty). This final phrase reminds us that His favor is vast beyond measure, sufficient for all who sincerely seek it, and that He alone determines how it is distributed. The door to His infinite favor remains open for anyone who approaches with genuine faith and commitment to His final Messenger ﷺ.
